---
title: "DICOM Services and PACS Architecture"
lang: en-GB
---

## DICOM Services — DIMSE: images in action  {#sec-dicom-dimse}

### Application Entities, SCU, and SCP

Beyond the data model covered in the previous chapter, DICOM also defines
how information is manipulated, through the definitions contained in
**DIMSE** (*DICOM Message Service Element*).

**DIMSE** is the services layer of the standard, specified in part PS3.7
[@DICOM_PS37].

The first concept to consider in any communications system concerns the
**entities** responsible for communication, i.e., **sender** and
**receiver**. In a DICOM system, all communication occurs between
**Application Entities** (AE). An AE is any device or application
that implements the DICOM standard: a CT scanner, a PACS archive server,
a visualisation workstation, or a printer. Each AE is uniquely identified
by an **AE Title** — an alphanumeric string configured by the system
administrator.

In each interaction, an AE assumes one of two roles:

- **SCU** (*Service Class User*) — the entity that requests the service,
  sending the request (`-Rq`). This entity may also be informally
  identified as the *client*.
- **SCP** (*Service Class Provider*) — the entity that provides the
  service, responding to the request (`-Rsp`). This entity may equally
  be informally identified as the *server*.

SCU and SCP roles are not fixed: the same AE may be SCU in one
interaction (when sending images to the archive) and SCP in another
(when receiving a query request from a workstation). This flexibility
is fundamental to the operation of distributed systems with multiple
devices [@DICOM_PS37].

DIMSE services follow a standardised naming convention: the prefix `C-`
indicates that the service operates on composite data (*Composite*); the
prefix `N-` indicates normalised data (*Normalized*). The suffix `-Rq`
identifies the request and `-Rsp` the response.

### Structure of a DIMSE message

Each DIMSE message is composed of two elements: a **DICOM Command
Object** and, optionally, a **DICOM Data Object**. The Command Object
is encoded as a set of group `0000` Data Elements — following exactly
the same TAG + VR + Value structure as the other Data Elements, but
defined in part PS3.7 (not in the Data Dictionary PS3.6). The most
relevant command fields are `(0000,0100)` (*Command Field*, which
identifies the service type), `(0000,0110)` (*Message ID*), and
`(0000,0800)` (*Data Set Type*, which indicates whether an IOD is
attached) [@DICOM_PS37].

The *Data Set Type* field has the value `0101h` when no IOD is attached
(as in C-ECHO) and another value when the IOD is present (as in
C-STORE). This distinction allows the receiver to know, before
processing the message body, whether or not to expect additional data.
The complete structure of a DIMSE message is illustrated in Figure 5.1.

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: structure of a DIMSE message.
  Left block: DICOM Command Object (group 0000) with fields.
  Right block: DICOM Data Object (IOD), optional.
  Arrow indicating that Data Set Type controls the presence of the right block.
  Note: if Data Set Type = 0101h, right block is absent.
  Teal palette.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 700 290" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:700px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="700" height="290" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- General title -->
  <text x="350" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">DIMSE Message</text>

  <!-- LEFT BLOCK: Command Object -->
  <rect x="20" y="32" width="290" height="220" rx="8"
        fill="#0a3d4a" opacity="0.06" stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="20" y="32" width="290" height="28" rx="8" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="165" y="51" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">DICOM Command Object (group 0000)</text>

  <!-- Command Object table rows -->
  <!-- Header -->
  <rect x="30" y="68" width="175" height="22" rx="3" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <rect x="210" y="68" width="90" height="22" rx="3" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="38"  y="83" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Element (TAG)</text>
  <text x="218" y="83" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Value</text>

  <!-- Row 1: Type -->
  <rect x="30" y="92" width="175" height="20" rx="2" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <rect x="210" y="92" width="90" height="20" rx="2" fill="#f8fbfc" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="38"  y="106" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">(0000,0100)  Command Field</text>
  <text x="218" y="106" font-size="10" fill="#374151">Store</text>

  <!-- Row 2: Message ID -->
  <rect x="30" y="114" width="175" height="20" rx="2" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <rect x="210" y="114" width="90" height="20" rx="2" fill="#f8fbfc" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="38"  y="128" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">(0000,0110)  Message ID</text>
  <text x="218" y="128" font-size="10" fill="#374151">24</text>

  <!-- Row 3: Priority -->
  <rect x="30" y="136" width="175" height="20" rx="2" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <rect x="210" y="136" width="90" height="20" rx="2" fill="#f8fbfc" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="38"  y="150" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">(0000,0700)  Priority</text>
  <text x="218" y="150" font-size="10" fill="#374151">Medium</text>

  <!-- Row 4: SOP -->
  <rect x="30" y="158" width="175" height="20" rx="2" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <rect x="210" y="158" width="90" height="20" rx="2" fill="#f8fbfc" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="38"  y="172" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">(0000,0002)  Aff. SOP Class UID</text>
  <text x="218" y="172" font-size="10" fill="#374151">1.2.840…</text>

  <!-- Row 5: Data Set Type — highlighted -->
  <rect x="30" y="180" width="175" height="22" rx="2" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <rect x="210" y="180" width="90" height="22" rx="2" fill="#1ab3a6" opacity="0.3" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="38"  y="195" font-size="9" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">(0000,0800)  Data Set Type</text>
  <text x="218" y="195" font-size="9" font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">≠ 0101h</text>

  <!-- Note 0101h -->
  <rect x="30" y="210" width="270" height="30" rx="4" fill="#fef9c3" stroke="#ca8a04" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="165" y="224" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#92400e">
    If Data Set Type = 0101h → no IOD attached
  </text>
  <text x="165" y="235" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#92400e">
    (e.g. C-ECHO carries no IOD)
  </text>

  <!-- RIGHT BLOCK: Data Object -->
  <rect x="390" y="32" width="290" height="220" rx="8"
        fill="#1ab3a6" opacity="0.06" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="6"/>
  <rect x="390" y="32" width="290" height="28" rx="8" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="535" y="51" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">DICOM Data Object (IOD) — optional</text>

  <!-- Data Object header -->
  <rect x="400" y="68" width="120" height="22" rx="3" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <rect x="525" y="68" width="145" height="22" rx="3" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="460" y="83" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Attribute</text>
  <text x="598" y="83" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Value</text>

  <!-- Data Object rows -->
  <rect x="400" y="92"  width="120" height="20" rx="2" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <rect x="525" y="92"  width="145" height="20" rx="2" fill="#f8fbfc" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="460" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0a3d4a">Patient Name</text>
  <text x="598" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#374151">Couto^Fernando</text>

  <rect x="400" y="114" width="120" height="20" rx="2" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <rect x="525" y="114" width="145" height="20" rx="2" fill="#f8fbfc" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="460" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0a3d4a">Patient ID</text>
  <text x="598" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#374151">987654</text>

  <rect x="400" y="136" width="120" height="20" rx="2" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <rect x="525" y="136" width="145" height="20" rx="2" fill="#f8fbfc" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="460" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0a3d4a">Modality</text>
  <text x="598" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#374151">CT</text>

  <rect x="400" y="158" width="120" height="20" rx="2" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <rect x="525" y="158" width="145" height="20" rx="2" fill="#f8fbfc" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="460" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0a3d4a">Rows</text>
  <text x="598" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#374151">512</text>

  <rect x="400" y="180" width="120" height="20" rx="2" fill="#e2f4f3"/>
  <rect x="525" y="180" width="145" height="20" rx="2" fill="#f8fbfc" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="460" y="194" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0a3d4a">Pixel Data</text>
  <text x="598" y="194" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#374151">[binary data]</text>

  <!-- Arrow between blocks -->
  <line x1="312" y1="191" x2="388" y2="191" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#ma)"/>
  <text x="350" y="185" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#1ab3a6">if ≠ 0101h</text>
  <defs>
    <marker id="ma" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- Caption -->
  <text x="350" y="278" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 5.1 — Structure of a DIMSE message: Command Object + optional Data Object (PS3.7)
  </text>
</svg>
</div>
```

### The four main services: C-ECHO, C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE

**C-ECHO** — verifies the existence of a functional DICOM connection
between two AEs. It goes beyond a simple TCP/IP `ping`: it confirms that
the DICOM application layer is operational. It carries no IOD. It is
routinely used to monitor the availability of system components
[@DICOM_PS37].

**C-STORE** — sends a complete DICOM instance from one AE to another.
It is the most widely used service in the clinical workflow: it occurs
immediately after each acquisition, when the equipment sends images to
the archive server. The equipment acts as SCU and the server as SCP.

**C-FIND** — queries studies, series, or images on a remote AE based on
criteria such as patient identifier, date, modality, or study description.
The SCU specifies the criteria; the SCP returns a list of matching
results. Note that C-FIND does not transfer images — only metadata
[@DICOM_PS37].

**C-MOVE** — requests the SCP to transfer images to a third AE. The SCU
specifies the destination by AE Title; the SCP uses C-STORE to send the
images to that destination. This indirect mechanism is typical of
visualisation workstations, which ask the server to send images directly
to themselves. Figure 5.2 illustrates the request–response model of the
three main services.

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: the four DIMSE services with SCU → SCP.
  Horizontal lines with arrows; request-response model.
  Teal palette.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:660px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="660" height="240" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>
  <rect x="20"  y="15" width="120" height="28" rx="5" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="80"  y="34" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">SCU</text>
  <rect x="520" y="15" width="120" height="28" rx="5" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="580" y="34" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">SCP</text>
  <text x="20" y="72" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#0d7c8c">C-ECHO</text>
  <line x1="80" y1="78" x2="520" y2="78" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a1)"/>
  <line x1="520" y1="88" x2="80" y2="88" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5" marker-end="url(#a2)"/>
  <text x="300" y="74" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0d7c8c">C-Echo-Rq (no IOD)</text>
  <text x="300" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">C-Echo-Rsp</text>
  <text x="20" y="128" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#1ab3a6">C-STORE</text>
  <line x1="80" y1="134" x2="520" y2="134" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a1)"/>
  <line x1="520" y1="144" x2="80" y2="144" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5" marker-end="url(#a2)"/>
  <text x="300" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#1ab3a6">C-Store-Rq + CT Image IOD</text>
  <text x="300" y="156" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">C-Store-Rsp (Success/Failure)</text>
  <text x="20" y="184" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#0d7c8c">C-FIND</text>
  <line x1="80" y1="190" x2="520" y2="190" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a1)"/>
  <line x1="520" y1="200" x2="80" y2="200" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5" marker-end="url(#a2)"/>
  <text x="300" y="186" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0d7c8c">C-Find-Rq (search criteria)</text>
  <text x="300" y="212" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">C-Find-Rsp (list of results)</text>
  <text x="330" y="232" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 5.2 — Main DIMSE services: request–response model (PS3.7)
  </text>
  <defs>
    <marker id="a1" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#0d7c8c"/></marker>
    <marker id="a2" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#94a3b8"/></marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
</div>
```

### SOP Class: the service–object contract

A **SOP Class** (*Service-Object Pair Class*) is the formal combination
of a group of DIMSE services with an IOD. It defines, normatively, what
can be done with a particular type of information object. Each SOP Class
is identified by a globally unique UID, registered in part PS3.6
[@DICOM_PS34; @DICOM_PS36].

The paradigmatic example is the **CT Image Storage SOP Class**: it
combines the C-STORE service with the CT Image IOD, identified by the
UID `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2`. When a CT scanner sends images to an
archive server, both devices negotiate during the connection establishment
(*Association Negotiation*) whether they support this SOP Class — the
transfer only occurs if both support it [@DICOM_PS34].

Note that an IOD is not necessarily an image with pixels: the
*Modality Worklist* IOD contains the list of pending examinations for a
modality, without any pixel data; and the *Verification* SOP Class
(C-ECHO) has no associated IOD at all. @tbl-sop presents representative
examples of normalised SOP Classes:

| SOP Class | IOD | Service |
|---|---|---|
| CT Image Storage `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` | CT Image IOD | C-STORE |
| MR Image Storage `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.4` | MR Image IOD | C-STORE |
| Study Root Q/R — FIND `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.2.1` | Study Root IOD | C-FIND |
| Study Root Q/R — MOVE `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.2.2` | Study Root IOD | C-MOVE |
| Modality Worklist `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.31` | Modality Worklist IOD | C-FIND |
| Verification `1.2.840.10008.1.1` | *(no IOD)* | C-ECHO |

: Examples of normalised SOP Classes [@DICOM_PS34; @DICOM_PS36]. {#tbl-sop}

It can thus be seen that DIMSE, IOD, and SOP Class have distinct but
complementary roles: DIMSE defines the message format; the IOD defines
the data structure; and the SOP Class declares the valid contract between
both, identified by a negotiable UID.

## PACS Systems {#sec-pacs}

### From analogue radiology to PACS

A **PACS** (*Picture Archiving and Communication System*) is the
computer system that manages the complete lifecycle of medical images
in a hospital institution: from acquisition at the modalities through
to archiving, distribution, and visualisation for diagnostic and
reporting purposes [@Huang2010].

The radiology workflow before PACS was entirely manual: patient
registration with the printing of a paper request; examination
performance and development of analogue film; manual search of the
physical archive to locate previous examinations; transport of dossiers
to the radiologist; dictation of the report; transcription; review and
signing; and insertion of the final report into the RIS [@Huang2010].

With PACS, this workflow is fully digitised: images arrive directly
from the equipment to the archive via DICOM C-STORE, previous
examinations are automatically pre-loaded onto diagnostic workstations,
the radiologist works in an integrated digital environment, and the
signed report is distributed electronically. The time between acquisition
and diagnostic availability drops from hours to seconds.

Documented benefits include the elimination of analogue film costs
(estimated at between 6 and 8% of the radiology budget in pre-PACS
hospitals), the reduction of repeated examinations due to inability to
access previous studies, and support for remote diagnosis
(*teleradiology*) [@Huang2010].

### General architecture and workflow

In a generic PACS, the **RIS** (*Radiology Information System*)
coordinates the administrative and clinical flow, whilst the DICOM
protocol transports images between the technical components. The
integrated workflow comprises:

1. The patient registers in the RIS, which assigns a unique identifier.
2. The RIS sends HL7 messages with examination data to the **PACS Broker**.
3. The PACS Broker notifies the archive server of the appointment.
4. Relevant previous examinations are pre-loaded onto diagnostic
   workstations (*prefetch*).
5. The examination room queries the DICOM **Modality Worklist** (C-FIND
   on the *Modality Worklist IOD*) to obtain the pending examination data.
6. The technician performs the examination; the equipment sends the images
   (C-STORE) to the quality control workstation (QC WS).
7. The QC WS forwards the examination to the radiologist's diagnostic
   workstation.
8. The examination is archived in the PACS server (database updated).
9. The server distributes the examination to clinical department
   workstations.
10. The radiologist dictates and signs the report; the database is marked
    as complete.
11. The report is transcribed and distributed electronically [@Huang2010].

### Architecture types: stand-alone, client-server, and web

Three fundamental PACS architectures can be identified, with different
trade-offs between resilience, flexibility, and network dependency
[@Huang2010]:

**Stand-alone (thick client)** — images are automatically distributed
by the server to diagnostic workstations, which maintain local storage.
The workstation can continue operating even if the server becomes
unavailable. The disadvantage is that each workstation has its own
*worklist*, creating a risk of duplicate reading of the same examination.
The *query/retrieve* function (C-FIND + C-MOVE) allows non-pre-loaded
images to be retrieved from the archive.

**Client-server (thin client)** — workstations have no local storage;
they access the central *worklist* directly and load images from server
memory. The *worklist* is single and shared, eliminating duplicate
readings. The structural disadvantage is that the server constitutes a
single point of failure (SPOF): any interruption prevents access to
images.

**Web-based** — images are served via HTTPS through the **DICOMweb**
interface (PS3.18), which exposes DICOM services as a REST API. Any
device with a compatible browser can access the archive without
installing specialised software. This model shares the network
vulnerabilities of the client-server approach, but adds platform
independence and natural support for teleradiology [@Huang2010]. Figure
5.3 compares the stand-alone and client-server architectures.

```{=html}
<!--
  Comparative diagram: Stand-alone vs Client-server.
  Two panels side by side.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:680px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="680" height="230" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>
  <text x="170" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Stand-alone (thick client)</text>
  <text x="510" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#0d7c8c">Client-server (thin client)</text>
  <line x1="340" y1="10" x2="340" y2="220" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="5"/>
  <rect x="130" y="35" width="80" height="36" rx="6" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="170" y="58" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#fff">PACS Server</text>
  <rect x="30"  y="110" width="80" height="30" rx="5" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="70"  y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff">WS + Storage</text>
  <rect x="130" y="110" width="80" height="30" rx="5" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="170" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff">WS + Storage</text>
  <rect x="230" y="110" width="80" height="30" rx="5" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="270" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff">WS + Storage</text>
  <line x1="170" y1="71" x2="70"  y2="110" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="170" y1="71" x2="170" y2="110" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="170" y1="71" x2="270" y2="110" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="170" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#16a34a">✓ Resilient to failure</text>
  <text x="170" y="178" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">✗ Worklist per WS</text>
  <rect x="470" y="35" width="80" height="36" rx="6" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="510" y="58" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#fff">PACS Server</text>
  <rect x="370" y="110" width="80" height="30" rx="5" fill="#e2f4f3" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="410" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0a3d4a">WS (no cache)</text>
  <rect x="470" y="110" width="80" height="30" rx="5" fill="#e2f4f3" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="510" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0a3d4a">WS (no cache)</text>
  <rect x="570" y="110" width="80" height="30" rx="5" fill="#e2f4f3" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="610" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#0a3d4a">WS (no cache)</text>
  <line x1="510" y1="71" x2="410" y2="110" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="510" y1="71" x2="510" y2="110" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="510" y1="71" x2="610" y2="110" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="510" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#16a34a">✓ Single worklist</text>
  <text x="510" y="178" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">✗ Server SPOF</text>
  <text x="340" y="215" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 5.3 — Architectural comparison: stand-alone vs. client-server (Huang, 2010)
  </text>
</svg>
</div>
```

### PACS integration with HIS and RIS

PACS integrates bidirectionally with the **HIS** (*Hospital Information
System*) and the **RIS** (*Radiology Information System*) through HL7
messages — a clinical interoperability standard addressed in a later
chapter. @tbl-integracao summarises the most relevant flows:

| From        | To          | Message / Service              | Protocol     |
|-------------|-------------|--------------------------------|--------------|
| HIS         | RIS         | Patient admission (ADT A01)    | HL7 v2       |
| RIS         | PACS Broker | Examination booking (ORM O01)  | HL7 v2       |
| RIS         | Modality    | Examination worklist (MWL)     | DICOM        |
| Modality    | PACS        | Acquired images                | DICOM C-STORE|
| PACS        | RIS         | Examination confirmation (ORU R01) | HL7 v2   |

: PACS–HIS–RIS integration flows [@Huang2010]. {#tbl-integracao}

The **Modality Worklist** (MWL) mechanism deserves particular attention:
it is through this mechanism that the acquisition equipment receives,
via C-FIND, the list of pending examinations. Without MWL, the
technician would manually enter the patient data into the equipment —
with risk of transcription errors. With MWL, data flows automatically
from the RIS to the equipment, ensuring that all generated DICOM files
already contain the correct metadata from the very first moment
[@Huang2010].

## Security in DICOM and PACS {#sec-dicom-seguranca}

### Normative mechanisms: CMS, TLS, and HTTPS

The DICOM standard has defined security mechanisms since 1999, driven
by HIPAA (*Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act*).
Specified in part PS3.15, they are organised in three layers [@DICOM_PS315]:

**CMS — *Cryptographic Message Syntax*** — operates at the DICOM object
level. It allows encryption of sensitive parts, notably patient data or
the *Pixel Data*. The protection is **permanent**: it persists in the
file regardless of the transport medium or destination system.

**TLS — *Transport Layer Security*** — operates at the TCP/IP connection
level during transfer via DIMSE. It creates an encrypted channel between
two AEs during the session. Protection exists **only in transit**: the
object at the destination is unprotected after reception, unless CMS is
also applied.

**HTTPS — Web Services** — when objects are served via **DICOMweb**
(PS3.18, REST API over HTTP), the standard mandates the use of HTTPS
for the transport layer. Like TLS, protection is restricted to the
communication channel [@DICOM_PS315].

The three mechanisms are not mutually exclusive: a robust implementation
combines CMS on objects with TLS on DIMSE connections and HTTPS on web
interfaces, covering data at rest and in transit. Figure 5.4 illustrates
the three layers and the scope of protection of each.

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: three DICOM security layers.
  Each block: coloured rectangle on top + descriptive labels below.
  CMS (object) | TLS (network) | HTTPS (web).
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:660px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="660" height="200" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- CMS -->
  <rect x="20"  y="20" width="190" height="130" rx="8" fill="none"
        stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="6"/>
  <rect x="45"  y="32" width="140" height="44" rx="6" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="115" y="59" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">CMS</text>
  <text x="115" y="95"  text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#0a3d4a">DICOM Object</text>
  <text x="115" y="111" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">(data at rest)</text>
  <text x="115" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#16a34a">✓ Permanent</text>

  <!-- TLS -->
  <rect x="235" y="20" width="190" height="130" rx="8" fill="none"
        stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="6"/>
  <rect x="260" y="32" width="140" height="44" rx="6" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="330" y="59" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">TLS</text>
  <text x="330" y="95"  text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#0d7c8c">DIMSE Connection</text>
  <text x="330" y="111" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">(data in transit)</text>
  <text x="330" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">✗ In transit only</text>

  <!-- HTTPS -->
  <rect x="450" y="20" width="190" height="130" rx="8" fill="none"
        stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="6"/>
  <rect x="475" y="32" width="140" height="44" rx="6" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="545" y="59" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">HTTPS</text>
  <text x="545" y="95"  text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#1ab3a6">DICOMweb</text>
  <text x="545" y="111" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">(REST / web)</text>
  <text x="545" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">✗ In transit only</text>

  <!-- Caption -->
  <text x="330" y="182" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 5.4 — DICOM security layers: CMS (object), TLS (network), HTTPS (web) — PS3.15
  </text>
</svg>
</div>
```

### Implementation responsibility

A critical aspect of the DICOM standard: **it defines the available
mechanisms but does not impose them**. The decision to apply security
rests with the hospital institution and the system vendor
[@DICOM_PS315].

In practice, many installations operate without active TLS on internal
DIMSE connections — acceptable in a segmented, controlled network, but
problematic with connections to external networks or in the face of
unauthorised internal access risk. The minimum alternative is access
exclusively via VPN; however, this approach does not protect objects
at the destination.

In the context of the **GDPR** and obligations for health data (special
category, Article 9), the absence of encryption on DICOM files
containing identifying patient data constitutes a legal and
organisational risk that institutions must actively assess and mitigate.

## Summary and Exercises {#sec-pacs-sintese}

### Consolidation exercises {.unnumbered}

1. A magnetic resonance scanner acquires a series of images and intends
   to send them to the archive server. Identify: (a) the DIMSE service
   used; (b) the SOP Class involved; (c) the SCU and SCP roles of each
   device.

2. Distinguish *Modality Worklist* from *Query/Retrieve*. At what point
   in the PACS clinical workflow is each used, and which DIMSE service
   implements each function?

3. Explain why the client-server model introduces a SPOF in the PACS
   and propose an architectural measure to mitigate this risk without
   abandoning the client-server model.

4. A hospital decides not to activate TLS on internal DIMSE connections,
   arguing that the hospital network is segmented. Evaluate this decision
   in light of the GDPR, considering that the DICOM files contain
   identifying health data (special category of data, Article 9).

5. The *Data Set Type* field of a DIMSE message has the value `0101h`.
   What does this value indicate to the receiver, and which DICOM service
   typically generates messages with this value?
